data.title: Verizon: A Strong Buy Due To Its Resilient Cash Flow And Stable Valuation
data.description:
In September last year I had recommended a strong buy for Verizon Communications (VZ). I was honestly looking for value and defensive stocks at that time, expecting some cooling in growth markets. I had positioned Verizon as an income-first vehicle, with a supportive regime ahead for share prices. Any rerating-led upside was optional for me. Verizon has lived up to that expectation very well with a total return of almost 9% since my Strong Buy call, at a time when the broader markets moved by only ~5%.

Verizon's strength in choppy markets toward the end of 2025 and in 2026 was very much visible (likely helped by some rotation away from growth into value/defensive names). At some point the rerating had pushed price appreciation alone to well above double digit returns, although recent corrections have coincided with a recent rebound in growth stocks (following some de-escalation signs in the Middle East).

Even after the round trip, share prices are still up by 2-3% since the time of my earlier thesis, while income has again done the heavy lifting as expected. Verizon continues to grind its way, outperforming the markets, and I expect more of the same as the upcoming quarters see the broader markets consolidate at best as AI trades cool further (earnings grow into valuations, no further immediate rerating till capex cycles and AI economics become clear enough to bet on long duration assumptions, and stagflation risks are watched closely). However, total returns are not part of my thesis - it is not what Verizon is designed for. I continue to look at it as an income vehicle - share price appreciation is optional; support here is sufficient to keep the income-led Strong Buy alive.

A review of numbers and what is happening structurally at Verizon reveals that valuations remain stuck at ~9x forward earnings (no rerating yet). Free cash flow has stayed strong and looks good ahead. A strong buyback outlook looks supportive for prices. Verizon has also discussed a major reshuffle with an eye on resetting its growth strategy - fixing churn from past pricing issues, shifting to value-led growth, cost optimizations, lowering capex, and improving fiber–wireless bundling. That could mean suppressed pricing boosts in the short term, but a durable and higher-quality revenue base over time (leaning on customer lifetime value, retention, and bundling). The upside, therefore, is still conditional through this transition (revenue growth in the 2-3% range and wireless flat), but importantly, the downside appears well-protected. That's all that matters to retain the Strong Buy from the income lens.

Price - Upside, Downside and Most Likely Path

The valuation story has not changed since September 2025 and has been extremely stable over the past few quarters, with the forward PE ratio at ~9x on average (and over the past 5 years). The valuation is at a discount to the sector median, but that does not immediately signal a scope for rerating, unless growth returns. Revenue growth was reported at ~2% YoY in Q4 2025 and 2026 is explicitly positioned as a transition year.

Growth quality also appears weaker. Verizon is currently avoiding price hikes and is even absorbing promotions to address churn (still looks elevated). That is not only a soft signal for increasing competition but also means the revenue outlook in the immediate future is not high quality growth. Both EBITDA and EPS were slightly under pressure in Q4 despite subscriber additions - a signal that markets should not be expected to be in a hurry to rerate Verizon soon.

What we can argue for with more conviction than an immediate rerating, is a support to valuations, given that recent multiple compression has rarely been sustained, especially below ~8.5x earnings - in fact Verizon has stayed extremely resilient (often rerated) even under broader market liquidity stresses.

The cash flow and earnings story looks better poised even as revenue growth is not impressive. EPS is guided to be up by 4-5% in 2026. This is not through an improved demand or pricing environment, though. The primary drivers here appear to be cost savings (~$5b) and lower capex (~$4b lower than 2025 to ~$16-16.5b). Free cash flow has remained stable over the last few quarters and is expected to grow steadily by 7% in 2026 (guided at over $21.5b). With around $11.5b paid in dividends (based on numbers in 2025), there is sufficient residual cash to enable buybacks (at least $3b in 2026 and up to $25b over the next 3 years) and still leave behind some for debt reduction or reinvestments. The cost optimization and lower capex now boost EPS mechanically over the next few quarters and keep boosting FCF expectations till the transition plays out and growth strength returns.

The deleveraging trend that was still improving in September has taken a turn, and the debt-to-equity ratio has increased marginally in Q4. However, the debt-to-equity ratio may show weakening even if changes in the equity denominator (due to buybacks, retained earnings, or acquisition accounting). A better measure is to look at net debt to EBITDA, which is currently at ~2.2x. Frontier is expected to add ~0.25x near term, before a return to the 2-2.25x range by 2027 (through EBITDA growth and steady deleveraging). Overall, balance sheet risks are not elevated, neither deteriorating significantly to affect the thesis.

The overall take on the share prices is one of watchful waiting as the revenue and growth reset takes place. I expect current valuations and earnings to be fully supported, if not rerated or improved significantly. That support comes in non-structural form - cost cuts and capex clampdowns, and even buybacks. However, FCF looks healthy through the reset - sufficient to support capital allocation to both debt and shareholders. The onus of total returns shifts to income and income alone, as a result.

Income - Sustainable and Growing

FCF coverage for dividends has already been discussed and remains as strong as in September. The outlook for 2026 is also well supported, with FCF growth expected over an already strong FCF in 2025. Importantly, this is after factoring in Frontier integration and continued investment, which makes the coverage more credible. Income is likely to keep thriving - recent dividend growth of ~$0.07 translates to a 2.5% increase (close to the 5-year CAGR growth rate). Overall, as long as investors look at Verizon as an income asset, the growth reset phase should not hurt them - we have already discussed how downside risks are low for prices from here, while the income angle is as strong as ever. That is what helps retain the Strong Buy rating - on a very strong carry trade.

I will keep an eye on how the revenue reset works out over the next few quarters starting Q1 2026. In terms of headline numbers, I do not expect thesis-altering developments from leverage, revenue, earnings, or free cash flow. However, how convergence (fiber and wireless bundling) is playing out will be the starting point to gauge execution toward the revenue reset (which could unlock rerating over the long term). Churn for bundled users is an important metric that should be tracked in the quarterly call (even if qualitative views). Another important area to monitor is how cleanly the ~$5b cost savings translates into margins (without hurting growth) - any margin expansion alongside stable or improving subscriber adds there could indicate that EPS growth is sustainable rather than temporary.

data.title: Sodexo: Guidance Cuts Erode Investment Case (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In our last update, we reiterated a buy rating for Sodexo S.A. (SDXOF) (SDXAY). This was due to a decline in the share price and lower guidance for 2026. Additionally, we were more pessimistic in our 2026 margin forecast. That said, we liked the company’s focus on the US, ongoing IT investments, and sector consolidation. Our buy rating was also supported by attractive valuations, along with support for dividend payments and potential buybacks. Thanks to strong free cash flow, we underwrote a positive investment case. With several of our initial assumptions no longer valid and following a 23% decline in the share price since we initiated coverage (Fig. 1), we decided to move our rating to Neutral. While this is not our preferred outcome, we believe a more cautious stance is warranted at this stage.

Fig. 1

H1 Results and Our Neutral Stance

Sodexo reported top-line sales of €12.1 billion, with organic growth of around 2%, but a change vs. H1 2025 of -3.7% (Fig. 2). Sales were -0.5% below Wall Street consensus, with underlying operating profit of €442 million, again -19% below consensus at €547 million. Sales growth was largely driven by pricing rather than volume, but was impacted by negative FX effects, particularly from the US dollar. On the GEO basis, results across regions were mixed. Europe was supportive thanks to new contracts, while North America, the group’s largest market, is under pressure due to prior contract losses and softer demand in key segments. This led to a lower operating profit margin that reached 3.7%, below expectations.

Fig. 2

Why are we now neutral?

At a high level, nearly all indicators show a decline versus the prior-year period.

First of all, almost every ratio is lower than the same period last year. However, on a 12-month view, top-line sales are now expected to be in the range of 0.5-1.0% (from a previous outlook of 1.5-2.5%). This reflects a weaker commercial momentum. More importantly, in our last update, we reported a larger cut in the EBIT margin, with a 30-basis-point contraction. We were not confident in the wording regarding a “slight” contraction. This left an unclear starting point, but we hope for procurement efficiencies and lower cost inflation. Considering that, we anticipated a core operating profit margin of 4.4%. With the current guidance, operating profit margins are expected to be between 3.2% and 3.4% (Fig. 3), while other operating expenses are expected to be -€300 million (down from a previous estimate of -€200 million). This is the second cut in guidance in a short timeframe, but the magnitude of the cut will likely lead to very material reductions in consensus EPS forecasts. These margins are expected to decline as the company continues to invest in operational improvements and IT infrastructure. This is not a new piece of evidence, but given a less favorable business mix, earnings are expected to decline.

Fig. 3

Additionally, the company's leverage is expected to be above the group's 1.0-2.0x Net Debt/EBITDA target. With lower EBITDA and a reversal of working capital, the company's net debt increased to 2.7x vs. 2.3x last year (Fig. 4). Therefore, in our view, this removes the previously anticipated buyback potential, despite solid FCF generation. This buyback (in a depressed share-price environment) was the catalyst we were hoping for. With a higher debt, even opportunistic M&A are likely less favorable. Compared to our previous forecast, there are also two key negative takeaways: a higher net financial cost expectation of €140 million and an effective tax rate that moved from 19.5% to 25.9%.

Fig. 4

Earnings Change and Valuation

In our last assessment, we anticipated sales of around €24.5 billion and an EBIT margin of 4.4%. This led to an EBIT of €1.08 billion. At this stage, Sodexo guides sales at 0.5-1.0% and an operating profit margin of 3.2-3.4%. Assuming this guidance, Sodexo's turnover and EBIT should reach €24.1 billion and €795 million, respectively. Considering the current interest expense (€140 million) and the higher effective tax rate, we estimate the net profit of €484.7 million. This results in an EPS of €3.3 with the current outstanding shares. Currently, the company trades at a P/E of 12x and, given the current net debt (€3.5 billion), at a 6.8x EV/EBITDA. While the company's screening is inexpensive on an EV/EBITDA multiple, the P/E ratio looks aligned with the company's historical average. That said, we had a margin of safety with the EV/EBITDA target multiple set at 8x previously, but given the company's new outlook, this has been significantly reduced. Again, this second consecutive guidance cut will weigh on Wall Street sentiment. Blending our valuation with a P/E of 10x (target price of €33 per share), and an EV/EBITDA of 8x (€51.2 per share), we set the Sodexo target price at €42.1 ($9.9 in ADR). Therefore, we moved our rating to neutral (the company's price is at €39.9 per share at the time of writing).

Risks

Sodexo's downside risks include negative FX (as seen in the H1 2026 results) and a deterioration in employment levels in Europe and the US. The new CEO increased the other operating expenses by €100 million. These are potential additional restructuring costs. As noted in our coverage initiation, the company engages subcontractors. While this allows Sodexo to serve as a single point of contact, service quality may suffer. Sodexo's main risks also include wage inflation and further increases in raw material prices, especially for food costs, which represent c50% of revenue.

Conclusion

Well, lower guidance, no upside from M&A, and no more buyback (given a higher leverage) limit our positive stance on the company. Compared to our last analysis, there is limited room for valuation upside. So, we prefer to remain neutral at this stage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lucid: Robotaxi Deal And BOM Cost Reductions To Drive Upside
data.description:
With the stock market vaulting up to new highs, my personal view is that right now is hardly a time for complacency in the stock market. I'm still emphasizing active trading and portfolio selection, with a focus on next-gen growth stories that are being ignored in the stock market today. The AI hardware/semiconductor trade, in my view, has gotten crowded and expensive; autonomous driving and EVs, meanwhile, are not.

Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), in particular, is looking quite appealing after its share price dropped ~30% since the start of the year (compared to a ~10% drop for Tesla and a ~15% decline in Rivian). Arguably, Lucid is the U.S. EV maker that has made the biggest strides in terms of closing deals and securing the path toward its next-gen mid-size vehicle, which is still slated to start production in the back half of this year.

I last wrote a buy article on Lucid in December, when the stock was trading at ~$11 per share. Of course, my buy call was ill-timed. But though I acknowledge that Lucid is a riskier endeavor that requires us to bet on its future product roadmap, a lot has moved in the positive direction for Lucid in the past few months, including signing major new partnerships. Trading at under a $3 billion market cap (versus $20 billion for Rivian and $1.2 trillion for Tesla), I believe the company's growth prospects are severely underappreciated, and I'm reiterating my buy call on this name.

Uber and the robotaxi market opportunity

To me, the largest recent announcement that Lucid made was an expanded partnership with Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER). Uber is starting to make major strides in robotaxis. It has a more measured approach to market entry (it has an active presence in Atlanta and Austin), behind the rollout of rivals like Waymo. At the same time, Uber just released new data that showcases that of all the autonomous/robotaxi companies, AV utilization is highest on Uber's platform. This makes perfect sense: Uber already has the largest rideshare network in the U.S., whereas Waymo requires a fresh new app download and is a distant competitor in terms of ride volume.

As shown below, Uber notes that AVs in service on the Uber network have 30% higher utilization/trips per day, while the Uber app offers an ETA that is 25% faster than its peers.

With this early lead captured, Uber has restated its intention of becoming the largest autonomous vehicle network in the U.S. by 2029. Of course, Uber isn't the AV technology developer, and it's striking partnerships to source AV supply. One of the biggest recent catalysts for Lucid is a recent (April 14) announcement from Uber that it would expand its commitment to Lucid. Uber is now investing $500 million into the company (up from $300 million previously) and has now committed to a mid-term order size of 35,000 robotaxi vehicles from Lucid, versus 20,000 vehicles in the original deal.

35k units may be small peanuts for an EV maker like Tesla, but it's a major deal for Lucid. For scale comparison, note that Lucid is only guiding to 25-27k production unit volumes for FY26. Lucid had always clearly occupied the high-end, luxury EV brand tier, but it was missing a top-tier commercial partner. In my view, the Uber deal allows investors to start seeing Lucid more in a light similar to Rivian, which has a similar commercial deal with Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) to supply 100k electric delivery vans by 2030.

Lucid has pinned the global robotaxi market at a very wide scope. Excluding China (and one of its main AV companies, Pony AI Inc. (PONY)), the company is only expecting ~20k robotaxi deliveries by 2027, at $5 billion in annual sales (~$250k ASP per unit). By 2035, it's expected to have $300 billion in annual sales and 590k units.

The company has released early details on its pure robotaxi product, which it's calling “Lunar.” The car is expected to hit 5.5-6.0 miles per kilowatt hour (versus 4.8 miles for a standard Tesla Model 3 today), reduce operating costs for rideshare platforms by 40%, and charge up to 200 miles in 15 minutes.

In other news, Lucid just appointed a new CEO: Silvio Napoli, the Swiss chief executive of elevator maker Schindler Group, which generated CHF 11 billion in revenue (~$14 billion) and CHF 1.1 billion in net profit in 2025 (~$1.4 billion), with the company's current CEO, Marc Winterhoff, moving down to a COO role. The fact that Lucid landed a major industrial CEO to lead Lucid's commercialization stage strengthens its path to success.

Cost reductions and extended liquidity runway

Of course, we also need to state the obvious: Lucid's robotaxi sales and even its mid-sized vehicle program (the first of three mid-size vehicles, the Lucid Cosmos, is expected to hit the road in 2027 after ramping up production this year) are still very future-looking. To assess the feasibility of these goals, we need to look into Lucid's ability to drive cost reductions and to keep its balance sheet padded with substantial liquidity until it can generate positive cash flow.

For FY26, Lucid is still expecting negative gross margins (most recently in Q4, the company delivered an -81% gross margin) as it's still a very small-scale manufacturer. But recently, the company also laid out a comprehensive list of all the drivers that can help bridge it to a positive profit. Robotaxi sales, which we discussed in the previous section, alongside software revenue, are expected to drive higher margins for minimal capex.

But the majority of the improvements in profitability will come from Lucid's ability to scale its bill of materials (BOM). Over a three-year timeframe, the company has identified an opportunity to slice down 30-40% of the BOM for Lucid Gravity, the company's current $79,900 luxury vehicle. Compared to the still-unreleased SUV on the company's mid-size platform (which will include three vehicles, including the announced ~$50k Lucid Cosmos), it believes it can reduce its BOM by 60-70%.

The other key consideration for Lucid is sufficient liquidity, and we think the company passes this test quite well. As of the end of FY25, the company had $4.6 billion in total liquidity, which consisted of $2.1 billion in cash and the remainder in undrawn debt facilities. This is enough liquidity for Lucid to stretch out to 1H'27, including the planned $1.2-$1.4 billion in capex for FY26.

We note that this liquidity balance does not yet include the recently announced $500 million from Uber, as well as an additional $550 million from the Ayar Third Investment Company, an affiliate of the Saudi sovereign wealth fund. We presume that this additional ~$1.05 billion of capital bridges Lucid until at least the end of FY27 as long as its current burn projections hold, through the initial planned launch of Lucid Cosmos.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, plenty of risk rides on a small EV maker that is only the third-largest in the U.S. Execution will be of tantamount importance, and there's no guarantee that Lucid will hit its targeted BOM cost reductions or its sales goals for the mid-size product. And while we note the Uber order for 35k Lucid robotaxis is certainly a signal of faith in Lucid's platform, we note Uber has a diversified list of partners it's enlisting in its AV rollout, including classic carmakers like Mercedes-Benz.

If Lucid doesn't perform to expectations in road testing, Uber could deepen its ties to a competitor.

That said, we think Lucid is appealing after a 25%+ slide this year, and its small <$3 billion market cap reflects an opportunistic buying window. Stay long here.
